data.title: Kestra Medical Systems: Momentum Has Returned
data.description:
In September, I concluded that growth had returned in the case of Kestra Medical Technologies (NASDAQ:KMTS), but that lots of margin work remained. After a hiccup in growth earlier in the year, this developer of an innovative cardiac device (vest) saw growth return, yet lots of margin work was still to be done.

Starting this fiscal year, the issue of growth has been overcome amidst a solid increase in first quarter sales, with the full year sales guidance hiked. However, margins require a lot of work, as operating losses (temporary) are on the increase amidst heavy sales & marketing investments, which for now appear as expenses.

This observation, and a huge rally in the share price, makes me very cautious here at current levels. I like the performance, but see a deteriorating risk-reward proposition here.

All About The Heart

Kestra Medical has been founded little over a decade ago, back in 2014, with a goal to provide therapeutic technologies for patients which suffer from cardiovascular disease.

After a long development process, the FDA granted approval for its ASSURE WCD device in 2021, what it calls a next-generation wearable cardiovascular defibrillator, in which shocks are administered when heartbeats get out of rhythm. This is all done by a wearable vest with sensors, constantly measuring heartbeats and rhythms, being able to administer shocks, if and when needed.

Commercial launch started in 2022 and up to the IPO the company provided these solutions to some 17,000 patients across 500 hospitals across the country, with that number having risen to over 25,000 patients here, as nearly 300 million lives are covered under insurance.

Such solutions are key, with some 400,000 cardiac arrests taking place every year, many of them going unnoticed. Speed is of the essence in all of this, with survival rates being around 90% when treatment is administered within a minute, with the survival rate dropping in a rather linear fashion with 10% per minute passing.

Valuation & Performance

Kestra went public back in March, with shares sold to the public at $17 per share, levels at which the operating assets (so net of net cash) were valued around $600 million.

That was a substantial valuation for a business which posted revenues of just $7 million in 2023, although that these quadrupled to $28 million in 2024. However, these came with operating losses of around $85 million. The company reported sales at $12.8 million in the first quarter of 2025, and $14.7 million in the second quarter, yet the issue was the guidance, calling for third quarter sales to be flattish at $15 million. Moreover, quarterly operating losses were seen around $20 million.

The combination of a $60 million revenue run rate, no sequential growth and operating losses trending at $80 million, made me cautious. This "image" was confirmed in April, as while third quarter sales were up 82% to $15.1 million, sequential revenues were essentially flat. Furthermore, an implicit fourth quarter guidance which called for revenues between $15.4 and $15.9 million was hardly inspiring as well.

Turning More Upbeat

In July, Kestra reported fourth quarter sales and while revenue growth slowed down to 71% to $17.2 million, these revenues were substantially stronger than guided for. Trading down to the $16 mark, the operating asset valuation came in at $600 million, similar to the IPO day (amidst incurred losses). This still valued the business at around 8 times annualized sales, while losses (adjusted for IPO expenses) trend around $80 million per annum on an operating basis.

The company issued a 2026 revenue guidance which called for a 42% increase in revenues to $85 million, which looks relatively okay I guess, but investors were left in the dark with regard to margin developments. Amidst still huge losses, uncertainty, being a one-product business, a wearable solution, and everything, I was leaning cautious.

Finding Momentum

After the issues soon after the public offering, shares of Kestra have been on a momentum run in the fall, having risen from $16 to $26 in the time frame of just a few weeks here.

The real momentum coincided with the release of the first quarter results by mid-September. Revenues rose by 52% to $19.4 million, showing a nice $2.2 million increase on a sequential basis, as the strong start to the year inspired the company to hike the full year sales guidance by three million, now seeing revenues in excess of $88 million. Gross margins have risen to levels in the mid-forties, with the company guiding for gross margins to hit the 70s in the coming years.

Operating losses however were reported at $28.8 million which is on the increase, in part explained by a $4.6 million stock-based compensation expenses and $2.9 million non-recurring new public company costs, which however seems quite recurring going forward.

The 51 million shares now have risen to $26 per share, pushing up the enterprise valuation to more than $1.3 billion, with that valuation including a dwindling net cash position of $160 million, for an operating asset valuation well in excess of $1.1 billion. With revenues seen where they are this year, sales multiples have expanded to 12–13 times, as the margins have suffered a setback, at least so it seems.

And Now?

In the time frame of just a few weeks, shares have risen 60%. This comes after a solid first quarter earnings report, prompting the company to hike the full year sales guidance. This now calls for average revenues around $23 million per quarter in the remaining three quarters of the year, suggesting that sequential growth is seen from here onwards.

So far the positive, as the headwind is that of higher expenses, in part the result of costs related to the public offering, I as I hoped for a bit of operating leverage becoming apparent now. This comes as operating losses now really trend at $100 million, with net cash holdings only sufficient to finance the current burn for some 1.5 years.

This is in part due to aggressive hiring of sales reps. These tend to be less productive in their early stages, of course, although that a rally in the stock market might trigger management into some opportunistic equity raises down the road here.

Growth is very important here and should be prioritized over margins, as it shows commercial traction of the products and stands at the basis of long term value creation and attractiveness as potential M&A target. Hence, I like what I see here, as the hiccup earlier this year seems to be a real hiccup.

Amidst substantial operating losses, but moreover the huge share price returns seen already, I do not feel inclined to chase the shares here, but am keen to keep a close eye on the developments from here onwards.

data.title: Eurozone Growth Beats Expectations By Holding Firm In October
data.description:
By Peter Vanden Houte

France remains the weak spot

The eurozone PMI beat expectations, rising from 51.2 in September to 52.2 in October, a 17-month high. The strong rebound in Germany made a significant contribution, but there was also robust activity outside the two largest economies, with the expansion the strongest we've seen in over two years.

France underperformed, with activity contracting, reflecting ongoing political uncertainty.

On a sectoral level, services led the improvement, with the PMI up to 52.6 and job creation at its fastest pace since June 2024.

Manufacturing: stabilization rather than recovery

The manufacturing PMI edged up to 50, signaling stagnation rather than growth. After a sugar boost from surging US imports in the run-up to the tariff announcements, European industry lost momentum, with weak industrial production figures for August. New orders stabilized in October, but staffing continues to decline.

Adding to the risks, the Dutch government’s takeover of Nexperia could trigger chip shortages and may weigh on output in the coming months.

ECB still "in a good place"

Inflation signals were mixed: input costs eased, but output prices rose at the fastest pace in seven months. Manufacturing raised prices for the first time in six months, while services price hikes accelerated compared to September. While energy prices should push inflation down in the coming months, the PMI doesn’t signal many underlying deflationary pressures.

All of this confirms that the European Central Bank is still in a "good place". While we cannot entirely exclude any additional rate cut(s), the PMI strongly points to stable rates for some time to come.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation, or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal, or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Apple Q4 Earnings Preview: $4 Trillion Market Capitalization Ahead
data.description:
AAPL FQ4 earnings report scheduled on Oct 30

I last covered Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL) on Sept 9 with an article titled “Apple Stock: Patience Is A Virtue”. The article was triggered by the latest data released by SensorTower about the monetization of its services and rated the stock as a hold. With its FQ4 earnings reported (ER) just around the corner, this article serves as a preview for the incoming ER. My thesis will be twofold. First, I will explain why I believe its market cap (MC) will reach $4T shortly after the ER and join the $4T club with Nvidia (NVDA). Second, I will explain why I expect robust service growth and continued share repurchases in FQ4 and forward.

As background, in Jan 2022, AAPL became the first company to reach a $3T market cap during intraday trading. In late 2024, its MC climbed to a record of $3.91 T as seen in the chart below. Then a range of macroscopic headwinds (mostly the trade conflicts started in early 2025) interrupted the appreciation of its MC, which currently hovers around $3.85 T, yet to surpass the previous peak.

Against this backdrop, I consider the FQ4 ER to be the immediate catalyst to push its MC above the previous peak and very likely past $4 T too. As a simple estimate, the chart below shows that consensus estimates expect an EPS normalized of $1.77 for the upcoming quarter, translating into a 12.7% QoQ increase. With this quarterly EPS estimate, the full FY EPS estimate is about $7.4 per share. With its current P/E (35.09x as of this writing) and the outstanding diluted shares, the estimated MC would be $3.92 T already by the end of the FY.

But the MC appreciation could be more and quicker than my above estimate suggests for a variety of reasons. Next, I will detail two of the most immediate catalysts that I expect to see in FQ4 ER: AAPL’s continued transformation into a service company and continued share repurchases.

AAPL FQ4: Transformation from a hardware company to a service company

Looking past the next quarter, the current consensus EPS estimates (see chart below) expect APPL’s EPS to grow at a compound annual growth rate (OTC:CAGR) of ~11.7% in the 7 years between FY 2025 and 2031. Under these forecasts, AAPL’s MC would surpass $4 trillion somewhere between FY 2025 and 2026 and reach $4.27 T by the end of FY2026. Again, this estimate assumes its current P/E ratios and also current share count.

I certainly see good growth drivers that can support the above EPS projections. Before every ER, I tend to see many analyses focusing on AAPL’s hardware sales (iPhone, Mac, et al). I certainly see the reason given how successful the company has been with its hardware. However, given the already enormous installed base and also the ongoing AI integration, I suggest we pay more attention to the growth of its services. In a nutshell, my prediction is that APPL will eventually become a service business instead of a hardware business. And judging by the progress so far, my assessment is that the transformation has been extremely effective. I certainly expect FQ4 ER to report further progress on this front and won’t be surprised to see the pace quicken in the years to come.

As a reflection, the following Trefis analysis shows the breakdown of AAPL’s services-related revenues and the projected growth. As seen, APPL’s Services business raked in a total of $96.2 billion for FY 2024 with the App Store being the largest contributor. Looking forward, total sales from its services are expected to grow to $150 billion, translating into an increase of almost 56% compared to the 2024 level, significantly outpacing the expected overall EPS growth for the company (which is “only” about 40% between FY 2024 and 2028 according to the consensus estimate above).

AAPL stock: large-scale share buybacks

The second immediate catalyst I expect from the upcoming ER is share buybacks, which can help to appreciate its MC faster than what the above consensus estimates suggest. As a background, AAPL has been a large buyer of its own shares for years and I see no sign of slowing down. As an example, the following chart shows its share repurchase in the past 5 years since 2021. During these 5 years, the company has been spending on average around $22 billion on share repurchase EVERY QUARTER. As a result, its outstanding shares (after adjusting for its split) has shrunken by almost 12% during this period (see the next chart below).

Because compounding works in a multiplicative way, not an additive way, such large-scale and consistent share buybacks can be very potent. As a simple illustration, the table below shows my back-of-envelope estimate for the impact of AAPL’s share repurchases. My estimates are based on the following assumptions:

  • I assumed that AAPL’s operation cash flow (OPC) to grow at an 11.7% CAGR, i.e., the same as its EPS growth rates from the consensus estimates quoted above.
  • I assumed AAPL to deploy a fixed percentage of its operation cash share purchases, and the percentage is taken to be 72.7% based historical averages.
  • Finally, I also assumed an average repurchase price of 32.2x of OPC, which is the OCF under current market conditions.

You can see that its MC would reach $4.4 T by the end of 2026, more than the $4.27 T implied by the consensus estimate above thanks to the double compounding from profit growth and share count reduction.

Other risks and final thoughts

A few other observations are particularly noteworthy from my above estimate. First, you can see that its total shares count would further shrink by another 10.8% in the next 5 years under these assumptions. In other words, every APPL shareholder would own about 10% more of the business without really doing anything (that is, besides waiting). Second, you can see that its OPC would grow by almost 95%, which is noticeably larger than the sum of the profit growth (~74%) and also the share buybacks (about 11%) due to the multiplicative nature of compounding.

In terms of negative risks, the valuation of the stock is currently quite elevated not only by absolute standard but also when compared to close peers. For example, when compared with other over-achievers in the Mag 7 group, Apple's current P/E of 35.1x is the third highest, only after Tesla and also Nvidia. Although for a company that systematically and aggressively buys back its own shares, a valuation contraction may not be all that bad. As mentioned earlier, my above estimates assume all future buybacks occur at 32.2x of its OPC. If the multiple compresses, the same amount of money can buy more shares back, which could be a net positive for shareholders (that is, for the staying shareholders). Besides valuation risk, the company – together with the overall tech sector – face some going macroscopic uncertainties. For example, on the tariff front, Apple incurred about $800 million in tariff-related costs in the June quarter. In the meantime, the trade situation between China and the US remains highly fluid. But these uncertainties are ultimately temporary in my model.

To close, this reexamination on AAPL is triggered by its FQ4 earnings reported. I consider the ER to report a few catalysts that could push AAPL’s MC to a new record. The top two catalysts I expect to see in the ER are the continued progression of its services and also share buybacks. Looking further down the road, I expect these catalysts to create a double-compounding effect in the long term given the multiplicative nature of compounding. These positives offset the valuation risks and near-term macroeconomic uncertainties, resulting in a well-balanced return/risk profile.

data.title: Merck Q3 Earnings Preview: Issues That Will Dominate The Agenda
data.description:
Investment Overview

Merck & Co., Inc. (NYSE:MRK), the New Jersey-based Pharma giant - the world's sixth largest by market cap, with a share price of $88, and market cap of $219bn (at the time of writing) - will announce its Q3 earnings on 30th October.

According to Seeking Alpha, analysts consensus is for revenues of $16.98bn, normalized earnings per share ("EPS") of $2.35, and GAAP EPS of $2.06. Last quarter, Merck beat on normalized EPS of $2.13, but narrowly missed on revenues of $15.8bn, and GAAP EPS of $1.76.

In Q2, Merck earned 56% of its revenues from its oncology division, whose revenues increased by 12% year-on-year, 14% from its vaccines division, whose revenues were down -36%, 3.4% from its cardiovascular division, whose revenues were up 117%, 5.4% from its Hospital Acute Care division, revenues up 2%, 2% from Virology, revenues down -2%, 4% from Diabetes, revenues down 1%, and 10.4% from Animal Health, revenues up 11%.

In its Q2 earnings presentation, the company shared its 2025 financial outlook as follows:

Let's begin our Q3 analysis in the only place we can begin - with Keytruda (pembrolizumab), Merck's, and the world's, best-selling drug, that faces patent expiration from 2028.

Merck: Moving On From Keytruda With Packed Oncology Pipeline

Speaking during a fireside chat at the Morgan Stanley annual Healthcare conference, Merck's Chairman, President and CEO Robert Davis introduced Merck as follows:

If you look at where we are as Merck right now, I would say we're really in a transformation. We're moving from being historically the KEYTRUDA company to increasingly really on a precipice of a wave of launches you're going to see coming -- to becoming a launch company and a much more diversified company, which are things we've been very focused on.

If you look, we've communicated over $50 billion of commercial opportunity by the mid-2030s. And that doesn't even include some of the more recent deals we've done, like the recent deal we did for Verona or the deal for EyeBio, which each of those are a multibillion-dollar opportunity.

It is certainly true that Merck has historically been the "Keytruda company." The PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor has been a best-in-class treatment for a range of solid tumor cancers for many years, and in Q2, by my calculation, it accounted for almost exactly half - $7.96bn - of Merck's revenues.

Keytruda's revenues are still growing - they were up 9% year-on-year in Q2. However, all good things must come to an end, and its patents are set to expire in 2028. Then, the market will likely become flooded with cheap, generic versions of the drug, and revenues will begin to decline by ~30% per annum.

It may be both tedious and unfair on Merck that every earnings call, fireside chat, and every analyst note published on Merck for the next three years will begin by asking about Keytruda, but Merck's shareholders may not need to despair about it.

AbbVie (ABBV), the pharma giant responsible for marketing and selling Humira, the immunology drug that was the previous global bestseller before its U.S. patents expired in 2024, has shown that it is possible to keep growing despite the loss of a "jewel in the crown" asset. After two "down years," the Pharma is guiding for $60bn of revenues in 2025, which is higher than the $58bn earned in 2022, the year before Humira's patent expiry.

That is largely thanks to AbbVie's ability to develop two drugs in Skyrizi and Rinvoq that are now dominant in all the same markets as Humira once was, but in Merck's case, with Keytruda the situation is more nuanced.

For starters, in September Merck reported that it had secured FDA approval for a subcutaneous version of Keytruda, Keytruda Qlex, in almost all of the same solid tumor indication that the original, intravenous formulation - some 38, in total.

Apparently, Merck believes it can shift 30% - 40% of patients currently using the intravenous version to the SubQ version within two years, and the SubQ version may convey an extended period of patent protection, allowing to continue charging a premium price.

There are complications - only patients using Keytruda as monotherapy may use the SubQ version, which takes a couple of minutes versus a half hour infusion, but if patients are using Ketruda as a combo with another IV drug, there would be no reason to switch to SubQ (so the theory goes).

Analysts believe Keytruda Qlex might earn $7bn of revenues by 2031, with the original also earning ~$7bn in that year, so it seems Qlex will only form part of the solution of how to move on from Keytruda, plus, the advent of PD-1 / VEGF inhibitors like Summit Therapeutics (SMMT) Akeso could (emphasis on could) supplant Keytruda as standard-of-care in key markets like lung cancer.

Besides Qlex however, Merck has plenty more oncology assets in its pipeline - according to CEO Davis:

We have now 80 Phase III clinical studies underway, 60 in oncology, all of which could be registration-enabling. So that's a massive Phase III pipeline. And as we said, that's driving 20-plus assets that are unique assets that we think almost all of which have blockbuster potential. So I'll just lay that out there.

But within oncology specifically, if you take the $25 billion, we talked about that there's over $25 billion from our oncology assets, and what is that made up of? It's made up of our antibody drug conjugate ("ADC") portfolio.

It's made up of our small molecule portfolio, mainly precision molecular targeting agents, our individualized neoantigen therapy with what we're doing with Moderna and our recently acquired T cell engager that we have as well. So that kind of is what's in that. It excludes the subcutaneous KEYTRUDA, excludes other assets.

The CEO suggested that half of that $25bn figure (made up of revenues of pipeline assets Merck expects to bring to market, $50bn in total) could be earned by ADCs, a much hyped new drug class that combines a targeted therapy and chemotherapy. The CEO name checked Sac-TMT (sacituzumab tirumotecan, or MRK-2870) as being involved in 14 different Phase 3 studies, for example. By my count, there are least 10 more oncology candidates in Merck's mid-to-late stage pipeline.

To summarize, then, I'd broadly agree that Merck's current pipeline, Qlex included, could one day form a $25bn per annum oncology franchise, and if we add a $5bn - $7bn contribution from Keytruda itself, we might speculate Merck can achieve what AbbVie did and offset all of the lost revenue caused by Keytruda's LOE.

I'd preach a little caution, however, as AbbVie achieved its goal with just two drugs, whereas Merck is potentially reliant on guiding as many as ten new drugs to market - will all of them succeed? Not only must they succeed, they must also outperform competing products - for example, apparently AstraZeneca (AZN) has a TROP-2 targeting asset (the same target as Sac-TMT) already approved to treat breast cancer, and targeting lung. Merck's oncology franchise is only five assets today, with three of those being partnered assets.

In its Q2 earnings presentation, Merck shared the following overview of its late stage pipeline, featuring multiple oncology drugs - I'd advise readers to pay close attention to any updates provided in the Q3 earnings release or call with analysts next week on progress.

Outside Oncology - Some Setbacks / Advances To Be Aware Of

Merck is obviously more than just an oncology company, but some recent developments have arguably been troubling - for example, sales of Gardasil, its flagship vaccine for HPV infections, tanked in 1H25, falling to $2.45bn, compared to $4.73bn in the prior year, which Merck puts down to declining sales in China. These lost revenues may not be recoverable long-term.

Winrevair, Merck's new pulmonary arterial hypertension drug, has gotten off to a slow start commercially (compared to analysts estimates), earning ~$600m of revenues in 1H25, and diabetes drugs Januvia and Janumet are no longer showing meaningful growth.

If Merck meets its 2025 guidance, revenue growth in 2025 will be negligible, as the company reported revenues of $64.2bn in 2024.

As such, the pressure on Merck to bring through new drugs is ramped up somewhat. Enflonsia, an RSV drug used to protect infants from respiratory syncytial virus, will challenge Sanofi's (SNY) Beyfortus for market share in that indication - Beyfortus earned $18bn of revenues last year.

Merck has turned to M&A to revitalise its portfolio also, completing the acquisition of Verona Pharma, and its Ohtuvayre maintenance therapy for Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease ("COPD"), for $10bn earlier this year. In May, Merck also completed the buyout of Eyebio in a deal that could eventually cost $3bn. Eyebio's candidate Restoret is progressing through a phase 2b/3 trial for diabetic macular edema (DME) - a hugely competitive market, it should be noted.

Tariff and Pricing Headwinds Likely To Crystallize In Q3

Looking at the current pharmaceutical landscape, Merck faces several headwinds whose impact is currently unclear, although we may learn more when Q3 results are release and discussed by management next week.

For example, the well publicized tariffs on international imports into the U.S. Merck does business in China, and is also partnered with Chinese biotechs on early stage GLP-1 drug programs, and with Japanese Pharma Daichii Sankyo on ADCs. That $22bn alliance met with a setback in May when Merck pulled its regulatory approval submission in the U.S. for therapy patritumab deruxtecan.

Drug pricing issues dominate the pharma industry agenda, with the Trump administration pledging to introduce a Most Favored Nation ("MFN") - Americans pay no more for drugs than whichever nation charges the least - policy, and setting a deadline for such prices to be introduced.

During his recent fireside chat, Merck's CEO stated that:

we started to shift our manufacturing footprints and have in place plans to really do U.S. production for U.S. sales. So I would say, as well as you can be prepared, we are prepared, depending on how the tariff situation evolves.

The trump administration has been threatening to impose tariffs specifically on pharma imports, although this will apparently be waived if companies commit to manufacturing in the U.S, as Merck has been doing, while pulling out of deals to invest elsewhere.

Ultimately, the MFN policy only affects Medicare and Medicaid drug pricing, and so far as I am aware, Merck (nor any other Pharma bar Eli Lilly (LLY)) has begun to adjust pricing either domestically or abroad, although we will doubtless get more color on this next week.

Concluding Thoughts - An Interesting Time To Be A Merck Shareholder - Nor Necessarily A Great Time To Buy

Merck stock is down 13% year-to-date, and 18% on a 1-year basis, but up 16% on a five-year basis. It is not an especially impressive return, compared to, e.g., its oncology rival AstraZeneca - up 26% year-to-date, or Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), up >30%, it is in fact amongst the worst. Personally, I am not sure Merck's Q3 earnings will provide much upside impetus.

While key assets like Gardasil struggle, Keytruda will once again do most of the heavy lifting, which, even though its patent expiry remains years away, might unsettle Wall Street. There are many unanswered questions around drug pricing, manufacturing, China, and some pipeline uncertainty - do Merck's many oncology pipeline assets have what it takes to supplant Keytruda?

I would necessarily say these risks are priced into Merck's current market cap valuation of $218bn, which is ~3.5x forward sales, and ~10x forward earnings, although those ratios do feel low enough that an upgraded guidance or a positive update from a regulatory standpoint could potentially lift the share price.

Merck's GLP programs - partnered with Chinese Pharma Hansoh - remain too early stage to deserve to be assigned any genuine value, in my view, but a positive update could change that.

My conclusion is that Merck is by no means a bad company to be invested in, and it pays a decent dividend yielding ~3.7% at the time of writing, but I am not confident Q3 earnings will drag it out of something of a share price slump.

For that to happen, it will take a successful commercial launch of Qlex, a couple of pipeline wins of significance, and perhaps some additional strategic M&A - the company reported a cash position of ~$8.6bn as of the end of Q2, and long-term debt of $34bn - not excessive by Big Pharma standards.

In my view, the waiting game (for Merck stock to rediscover upside) will not end with next week's Q3 updates, but it will likely end eventually.

data.title: Games Workshop: Excellent Fundamentals But High Valuation (Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

This is the third article I've written about Games Workshop (OTCPK:GMWKF), the last one dating back over a year. Although the previous articles are dated (the first one dates back over two years) the content remains valid, and I've delved into the company's business in detail. I'll avoid repeating myself and focus solely on the important changes that have occurred and on updating my valuation. If this is your first time hearing about GAW, I invite you to read my previous two articles.

Business and Financial Updates

Since I last wrote about Games Workshop, there have been no significant changes in the company's business and strategy. Management has continued to execute with extreme diligence and effectiveness, delivering outstanding results for the company.

In my last article, we were left without any news regarding the agreement with Amazon. Exactly one month after I published the article, the deal was finalized. However, the production process is long and complex, and we'll have to wait several years before seeing a product released to the public.

The mass market presentation of the Warhammer 40,000 IP via the (very) popular animation Secret Level episode five: And They Shall Know No Fear and the video game Space Marine 2 certainly helped us - we had more visitors to our own stores at that time and we sold products relating to our IP that appeared in the show and the game

source: CEO Kevin Rountree Annual Report FY25

As for video games instead, Space Marine 2 continued its initial success, reaching over 7 million copies sold as of June 2025. The unexpected success prompted the production company Saber Interactive to announce in March the development of the third chapter of the saga, it is too early to speculate on the possible release date, as we are talking about several years of development before seeing the finished product on the market.

Games Workshop’s Licensing revenues therefore surprised and exceeded analysts’ expectations, with revenues amounting to £52 million in FY25, up almost 68% on the previous year.

Core revenue grew approximately 14%, supported by excellent performance in the Core segment and operating profit was up 60%, primarily thanks to the exceptional performance of the Licensing segment. One of the news items impacting the company's business are the new tariffs imposed by the Trump administration; the United States is an important and rapidly growing market for Games Workshop. The company stated that the impact of tariffs for the next fiscal year will be approximately £12 million, or 2% of gross margin, and also announced that an efficiency plan is underway to recover these increased costs, which, however, could take more than a year to complete.

In terms of geographic markets, North America continues to perform well, with growth of approximately 15% compared to FY24 and the opening of the 200th Warhammer store. Asia, the geographic area with the highest future growth potential, performed well, growing by 20% and opening the first Warhammer store in South Korea. However, the CEO specified that much more can be done in this region, especially in China:

If I’m honest our sales via our trade channel in China was behind our internal growth target due to resourcing issues. It was still over 20%, but the potential is significantly more. We should have added more support quicker, we are fully staffed now. We are also investing in the senior manager structure in this territory.

Finally, management's capital allocation prowess remains undisputed, with an impressive ROCE of 191% in 2025. Investments have been announced to expand production capacity for both miniatures and paints, which will impact returns next year; however, they are crucial to the company's long-term growth.

Valuation

Few analysts provide estimates for Games Workshop, and it remains difficult to accurately estimate future results. In particular, the greatest difficulty lies in estimating earnings, given that they are significantly influenced by the results of the Licensing segment, whose results are not easy to estimate.

For 2026, I will use the estimates from Edison Investment Research, the firm directly engaged by the company to provide the forecasts. Revenue and PBT are expected to decline in 2026, given that the Licensing segment will not achieve the same incredible results as in 2025. More specifically, a 6% decline in revenue is expected. However, I would like to point out that Edison's estimates have proven conservative in previous years and have been consistently beaten.

Three analysts provided a slightly higher average estimate for 2026 revenue, around £605 million. In the base and worst-case scenarios, I used Edison's 2026 estimates, while in the best-case scenario, I assumed revenue of £590 million, midway between the two estimates. The drivers of the company's long-term growth have not changed from what I discussed in my previous article, and I believe Games Workshop has many levers it can use to continue growing.

I've assumed a payout ratio of 80%, and from the estimates you can see, the company currently appears overvalued even in the best-case scenario. The company's recent successes have raised expectations and, consequently, its valuation; however, there hasn't been any news to suggest these expectations are unjustified. After all, the potential stemming from the development of Space Marine 3 and the Amazon deal is real, and it's therefore appropriate to take it into account. However, it's also true that the price currently offers little margin for error, and if enthusiasm for the Warhammer universe were to wane, the current price would appear excessive.

Conclusion

With this article, I wanted to maintain continuity in my coverage of Games Workshop, continuing the work I've done in previous articles. I wanted to discuss the developments I deemed most relevant for the company's future. As you've read, nothing earth-shattering has happened; management has simply continued to execute the strategy it has been pursuing for over 20 years and has sought to best respond to external events such as tariffs.

In my last article on Games Workshop, I gave the company a "Buy" rating, and about 10 days later, I took advantage of a slight drop in the share price to make an initial investment. I am therefore currently a shareholder in GAW, and rate it as a "Hold." While there may be doubts about the current valuation, especially when compared to the company's potential short-term results, I have no intention of selling, given that the long-term fundamentals have only improved since I invested in the company.

However, I have no intention of increasing my position at these prices, given the slim margin of error that current valuations offer. If the market is disappointed by the results over the next year, primarily due to licensing revenues not being as high as they will be in 2025, I will be happy to increase my position at more favorable prices. What motivates me to remain invested is that my purchase price is consistent with the valuation of my base case, and I believe the scenario described by the best case is not that difficult to achieve. Furthermore, it is rare to find companies of this quality and so impeccably managed.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Ford Motor Q3: Messy Guidance Meets Mild Implied Volatility - Buy Options
data.description:
F stock: Q3 earnings recap

I last covered Ford Motor Company (NYSE:F) stock on July 31 in an article entitled “Ford Earnings: Why And How I Plan To Sell At $11”. That article was triggered by the elevated implied volatility (IV) at that time, which created attractive option premiums for options sellers. The article rated the stock as a hold and suggested investors consider the use of covered options.

Since then, there have been a few new catalysts evolving around F stock. In the remainder of this article, I will concentrate on the top 2 as I see them: the FWD guidance the company issues in its Q3 earnings report (ER) and also the changes in its IV in the option market. As will be elaborated later, these changes have led me to see the opposite situation to what I saw in my last writing. In a nutshell, I believe the option market is currently underestimating the true uncertainties the company is facing, thus creating an attractive pricing environment for option buyers.

For readers unfamiliar with the Q3 ER, the screenshot below provides a summary. Overall, my impression is that the guidance is quite messy with many moving parts embedded. Notably, the company expects around $0.7 bn of adverse net tariff headwinds ahead and $1.5bn-$2.0bn impact from the Novelis fire. Novelis is a critical aluminum supplier for Ford. The fire could disrupt the supply of this critical material for the production of many of F’s vehicles, including some of its most lucrative models in the near future. The company’s updated guidance points to full FY25 EBIT in the range of $6.0bn-$6.5bn, which is quite weak in my view. The combination of the Novelis and tariff uncertainties also made me skeptical about the odds for this guidance to actually materialize.

Yet, the IV for F stock is quite mild in the options market, which does not represent the true uncertainties in my view, as elaborated on next.

F stock: yet implied volatilities are mild

To wit, the following table (taken from MarketChameleon) shows the statistics of F’s IV in the past year. As seen, the current reading is 28.8, noticeably below its historical average of 33.0 during the past 52 weeks. F’s current IV is in the 42% percentile rank, meaning that 42% of the time the IV was lower in the last year than the current level. Furthermore, the current IV is -22.3% below its 20-day moving average of 37.1 and also is -11.6% below its 20-day historical volatility of 32.6 (HV).

The contrast of such mild IV and the fundamental risks creates a favorable setup for option buyers in my view. More specifically, it provides a way for options buyers to benefit from the actual stock price moves with a fraction of the capital layout required for direct share ownership.

As a specific example, the following chart (taken from Seeking Alpha’s option chain data) shows an F call option with an expiration date of Jan 16, 2025, which is roughly 3 months from now and thus can capture most of the business uncertainties mentioned before the company’s next ER. More specifically, for the NTM (near-the-money) call option with a strike price of $14 (the stock currently trades at $13.5 as of this writing), it last traded at a premium of $0.53 per share only. The premium is thus about 3.9% of the current share price. Buyers of these calls thus can gain exposure to F shares with a small fraction of the capital layout compared to direct share purchase. Similarly, exiting F investors could consider buying F put options as a cost-effective insurance against downward volatilities caused by the risks discussed above.

Finally, also note these options are quite liquid, which can facilitate near-term trading. The option with the $13.00 strike price has a volume of 4805 contracts and an open Interest of 38,131 contracts. The $14.00 strike price option has a volume of 6224 contracts and an open Interest of 1271.

Other risks and final thoughts

My earlier discussion of the risks focused more on the magnitude of their financial impacts. Here, let me focus on the timing of these risks. Based on the information provided in the ER, my guess is that the aluminum plant could return to production in the time frame between November and early December 2025. Ramping to full production could take longer, most likely in Jan 2026. This expectation adds another reason for investors to consider options with expiration dates sometime in Jan 2026. I expect updates on the aluminum plant to be announced during this period. The direction of these updates could cause large price swings for F shares, and the options will help investors (that is, buyers) to benefit from and/or hedge against these updates.

The current valuation of the stock represents another uncertainty that options could help to hedge. As you can see from the chart, the stock is priced at a single digit P/E on a TTM basis (of around 9.1x), which is quite low in absolute terms or when compared to the sector. However, when compared to the stock’s historical norm, the current evaluation is quite high. To wit, its Non-GAAP P/E (TTM) of 9.14 represents 43.34% discount to the sector median of 16.13. However, it is 26.69% higher than its own five-year average of 7.22. The P/E premium in FWD terms is even larger, more than 39% above its historical average.

All told, this follow-up article on F is triggered mainly by two factors: the FWD guidance issued in its Q3 ER and the changes in its IV. These factors led me to see an attractive pricing environment for option buyers. The use of options is part of the strategies we use in our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement) for both hedging and also leveraging purposes. Readers new to the use of options should be aware that our above risk calculus is performed in terms of the total amount of capital exposure relative to direct share ownership. In terms of percentages, option buyers face the risks of 100% total loss if the options expire worthlessly. In the case of F, our verdict is that the current IV is an underestimate of the true uncertainties, thus creating an attractive setup for buyers.

data.title: AIER's Everyday Price Index Inches Up Again In Sept. 2025
data.description:
By Peter C. Earle

Our proprietary index climbed for the tenth month in a row in September 2025, but there are clear signs that price pressures are finally subsiding.

The AIER Everyday Price Index (EPI) rose 0.29 percent to 297.6 in September 2025, marking its tenth consecutive monthly increase and bringing the year-to-date change to roughly 3.2 percent. Out of the 24 components, 15 categories posted price increases, one was unchanged, and eight declined. The strongest gains came from gardening and lawncare services, motor fuel, and intracity transportation, reflecting both seasonal and energy-related pressures. Offsetting those, the most notable price declines occurred in nonprescription drugs, admissions to movies, theaters, and concerts, and housing fuels and utilities, indicating modest relief in select consumer essentials.

AIER Everyday Price Index vs. US Consumer Price Index (NSA, 1987 = 100)

Also on October 24, 2025, the US Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) released its September 2025 Consumer Price Index (CPI) data. The month-to-month headline CPI rose 0.3 percent while the core month-to-month CPI number increased by 0.2 percent, both of which were lower than forecasts by 0.1 percent.

September 2025 US CPI headline and core month-over-month (2015 – present)

In September 2025, the food index rose 0.2 percent following a 0.5 percent increase in August, with food-at-home prices climbing 0.3 percent as four of six major grocery categories posted gains, including other food at home (0.5 percent), cereals and bakery products (0.7 percent), nonalcoholic beverages (0.7 percent), and meats, poultry, fish, and eggs (0.3 percent), while dairy products declined 0.5 percent and fruits and vegetables remained unchanged, and food away from home increased a modest 0.1 percent. Energy costs surged 1.5 percent in September, accelerating from August’s 0.7 percent rise, driven primarily by gasoline prices that jumped 4.1 percent, while electricity fell 0.5 percent and natural gas dropped 1.2 percent.

Core inflation, excluding food and energy, moderated to 0.2 percent in September from 0.3 percent in each of the previous two months, with shelter costs rising 0.2 percent as owners’ equivalent rent posted its smallest monthly gain (0.1 percent) since January 2021.

Also in the core numbers, transportation costs were mixed, with airline fares increasing 2.7 percent and new vehicles rising 0.2 percent as motor vehicle insurance and used cars and trucks both declined 0.4 percent. Other notable changes included recreation and household furnishings each rising 0.4 percent, apparel gaining 0.7 percent, personal care up 0.4 percent, and communication falling 0.2 percent. Medical care costs increased 0.2 percent overall, with hospital services and prescription drugs each rising 0.3 percent, offsetting declines in dental services (down 0.6 percent) and physicians’ services (down 0.1 percent).

Tracking changes over the previous 12 months, both the headline and core Consumer Price Indices rose 3.1 percent, slightly higher than the 3.0 percent rise that was projected for both.

September 2025 US CPI headline and core year-over-year (2015 – present)

Over the 12 months ending in September, overall food prices increased 2.7 percent, with grocery prices holding steady from August while dining-out costs accelerated to a 3.7 percent annual pace. Within food categories, meats, poultry, fish, and eggs climbed 5.2 percent, and nonalcoholic beverages rose 5.3 percent, while “other food at home” advanced 1.9 percent and cereals and bakery goods were up 1.6 percent. Energy prices rose 2.8 percent over the year, led by steep gains in electricity (5.1 percent) and natural gas (11.7 percent), though gasoline edged 0.5 percent lower. Full-service restaurant meals rose 4.2 percent compared with 3.2 percent for limited-service meals, while fruits and vegetables gained 1.3 percent and dairy prices were up a modest 0.7 percent.

Core CPI, which excludes food and energy, increased 3.0 percent year-over-year, driven primarily by shelter costs, which advanced 3.6 percent. Other notable contributors included household furnishings and operations (4.1 percent), used cars and trucks (5.1 percent), medical care (3.3 percent), and recreation (3.0 percent). These figures highlight that while goods inflation has moderated, services and housing remain the key sources of upward price pressure within the core index.

The September 2025’s Consumer Price Index report delivered news of a welcome moderation in inflation, marking the slowest pace of underlying price growth in three months. Core CPI was restrained by a cooling in shelter costs — the smallest increase in owners’ equivalent rent since early 2021. Broader price movements were similarly tame: goods inflation eased on cheaper used cars and slower gains in household furnishings, while services inflation was capped by softening rents and airfare costs. Of note, the data release was delayed by the ongoing federal shutdown and assembled primarily to ensure the Social Security Administration could calculate its 2.8 percent cost-of-living adjustment for next year.

For policymakers at the Federal Reserve, the report reinforces confidence that price pressures are continuing to cool without threatening broader economic stability. The slower pace of inflation, particularly across shelter and core services, effectively seals the case for a 25 basis point rate cut at the late-October Federal Open Market Committee meeting and strengthens the likelihood of another in December. The immediate financial market reaction reflected that view: Treasury yields and the dollar slipped, while stock futures advanced. Despite persistent tariff exposure — particularly in categories like apparel and household goods — the overall pass-through to consumers remains modest. Estimates suggest firms passed through roughly 26 cents of every dollar in new tariff costs, underscoring how competitive pressures and slowing demand are muting inflation’s reach.

Still, the data paint a nuanced picture beneath the headline calm. Measures of inflation breadth show that while fewer items posted outsized increases, nearly half of core CPI components continue to rise at an annualized rate above 4 percent, signaling lingering while narrowing stickiness. The primary concern now is not so much inflation’s direction but data continuity: with the government still shuttered, the Bureau of Labor Statistics has suspended most data collection, casting doubt on the release and accuracy of upcoming CPI reports. For the moment, though, September’s figures offer reassurance that inflation is on a slower glide path — enough to justify the Fed’s easing bias but not yet soft enough to rule out renewed vigilance if tariff or supply shocks reemerge over the next several months.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: On Holding: A Bottom Is In Sight
data.description:
Looking at On Holding’s (NYSE:ONON) share price performance in the last few quarters, one would think that the business is doing poorly. That is certainly not the case, and yet, the stock is down 16% since my previous update in October 2024. In the article, I argued that the company remains well-positioned in the long run, but that the valuation is near the high end of the historical range, that the near-term upside may be limited, and that we may see the stock correct or consolidate in the following quarters.

Fast forward 12 months, and the stock is at the low end of its historical valuation range, and at levels where I see the risk-reward as far more favorable than last October.

Tariffs and peer pressure

The real reason the stock is down in the last few quarters is the very poor peer group sentiment. With a 9% share price decline in the last 12 months, On is the best-performing stock in its peer group.

The obvious reason for the poor sentiment is tariffs as these footwear companies are very dependent on manufacturing in Asia, and these countries have been subject to tariffs imposed by the White House. Tariffs on goods from Vietnam, Indonesia, Cambodia, Bangladesh, and other countries where factories of these companies are located are generally in the 30s and 40s, and this causes both concern among investors, and has resulted in a real economic impact on these companies.

On Holding has, so far, handled the situation quite well. In fact, the company has increased its full-year gross margin guidance from 60.0-60.5% to 60.5-61.0% when it reported second quarter results. This guidance includes additional incremental tariffs versus previous expectations, and the higher gross margin should be achieved through a combination of price increases, increased full-price sales, improved operational efficiency, and a positive exchange rate impact.

On’s growth rates have improved in the last few quarters

In addition to the improved gross margin, On's business has returned to stronger growth. This is in line with the company's promises a few quarters earlier, as the growth slowdown was caused by the reorganization of the EMEA business with reduced doors across the region due to the company's focus on profitable growth.

In the chart below, I included both the reported Y/Y growth rates in the last 8 quarters in Swiss francs, and the growth rates in U.S. dollars, as the strength of the Swiss franc has negatively impacted the reported growth rates (with some fluctuations).

The growth in other regions (North America and Asia-Pacific) was consistently strong throughout this period, and the promised rebound in EMEA region's growth rates has arrived. The growth is broad-based with increasing contribution from new markets such as France and Italy, and established markets like the U.K.

I was also pleased to see the growth acceleration of the Asia-Pacific region, which I felt had more room for percentage growth rates considering the very small revenue base. Management said on the Q2 call that demand is outpacing supply in all APAC markets with 50% Y/Y same store growth in On-operated stores in China.

The DTC (Direct-To-Consumer) channel continues to rise in importance, and the strategy of expanding self-operated stores is starting to deliver, although the number is still quite small with 54 On-operated stores globally versus 11,000 doors through the wholesale channel.

Apparel remains a tiny category, but it is also making progress with management reporting deepening consumer engagement.

Management has increased the full-year revenue growth guidance from 28% to 31% in constant currency based on the strong momentum in the first half of the year. The increased guidance is not visible if we look in Swiss francs, as the company started the year with full-year revenue guidance of CHF2.94 billion, and then moved it down to CHF2.86 billion in Q1 and then increased it to CHF2.91 billion in Q2. In U.S. dollars, this represents an approximate $300 million increase in the full-year guidance.

Overall, I am very pleased with the progress over the last 12 months.

Risk-reward a lot more favorable based on the improved growth outlook and valuation near the low end of the historical range

At the time of my last update, On was trading in the low 50s and I argued that the upside in the near- and medium-term is likely limited as the stock was trading near the high end of its historical range (I was looking and continue to look at the price-to-sales ratio as the preferred method for high growth companies), and within my $52-64 valuation range. Coincidentally, the stock has peaked around $64 per share in January.

Today, we are looking at a very different picture. The stock is now at the low end of the historical valuation range.

I am increasing my valuation range from $52-64 to $65-74 per share on passage of time adjustments and increased revenue expectations. My 2027 revenue estimate range has increased from $4.8-5.0 billion to $5.4-5.5 billion (and this is slightly below the $5.65 billion consensus), based on a weaker U.S. dollar better-than-expected execution.

Conclusion

On Holding is weathering the tariff storm very well. The risk-reward has improved considerably since last year as margins and the growth outlook have improved, and as the valuation is near the low end of its historical range.

data.title: American Airlines: Rough 2025 Ending On A High Note
data.description:
In no huge surprise, American Airlines Group Inc. (NASDAQ:AAL) reported a better Q3 than the company had forecast. Travel demand has remained strong, and legacy airline peers had already reported strong results for the September quarter. My investment thesis remains ultra Bullish on American Airlines, still trading towards the multi-year lows while the long-term catalysts remain in place for higher growth.

Big Q3 Beat

American Airlines beat Q3'35 earnings estimates, but the company generally reported a disappointing quarter as follows:

The airline beat Q3 EPS estimates by $0.11, but American Airlines still reported a sizable loss for a typically profitable quarter. As an example, the company earned $0.30 per share last Q3, so a $0.17 loss is a huge dip from last year.

A peer like Delta Air Lines (DAL) generated a Q3'25 operating margin of 11.2%. Delta Air Lines has a far better cost structure and doesn't face the same wild quarterly swings.

American Airlines should benefit from the struggles at Spirit Aviation (OTC:FLYYQ). The legacy airline has 138 route overlaps with Spirit based on cities, higher than the other legacy airlines.

The company didn't even discuss Spirit Airlines on the Q3'25 earnings call, though the improving domestic yields could be partly contributed to the ULCC struggles. On the Q3'25 earnings call, CFO Devon May discussed improving yields in the Main Cabin, possibly tied to Spirit cutting back service, as follows:

Domestic year-over-year PRASM improved sequentially each month and turned positive in September. While premium continued to outperform Main cabin, we've seen improvement in the Main cabin since its low point in July. That momentum has continued into October, and we're encouraged by the bookings we have taken for November and December.

American Airlines forecasts Q4 EPS of $0.45 to $0.75, above consensus estimates of $0.32. More importantly, the strong number isn't actually stronger than the $0.86 earned last year, so the airline has a long way to go to recapture even the 2024 profit levels.

Catalysts On The Way

As with the whole legacy airline sector, American Airlines is in line to continue generating growth via their brand loyalty program. The airline has already seen active AAdvantage accounts grow 17% over the last 2 years, while the company has a new exclusive deal with Citigroup (C) starting on January 1. The program promises to boost revenues 10% annually to $10 billion by the end of the decade with a massive $1.5 billion boost to operating income compared to 2024 levels.

American Airlines only generated operating income of $3.3 billion last year, and the forecast is now for substantially less this year. The airline has long promised this opportunity to vastly boost profits, but the company is still forecasting something below a $1 EPS this year after hitting nearly $2 back in 2024.

Depending on tax rates at the time, American Airlines would see a nearly $2 EPS boost from $1.5 billion in additional operating income. The company has ~720 million in diluted shares outstanding.

The airline is still spending ~$342 million in quarterly interest expenses. As the story has been for a while, American Airlines is still holding excessive cash at ~$7.6 billion with net debt just above $20 billion. The U.S. cutting interest rates more will benefit the airline more than the others with the reduction in interest expenses to where the combination of the loyalty award boosts and lower interest expenses could add $2 billion in pre-tax income, or roughly double the 2024 levels when the EPS was $2.

American Airlines is still struggling to consistently report profits similar to legacy airline peers due in part to the fatal crash and the business account snafu. The company could run into similar problems in 2026, but the opportunity is for the airline to finally fully execute and return to pre-Covid levels, similar to Delta and United Airlines (UAL).

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that American Airlines is ending 2025 on a high note after a big setback for the year. The airline still has the major catalysts and the potential benefits of Spirit Airlines pulling back service in key markets. If American can repeat the 2024 financials, the stock heads much higher.

data.title: Hensoldt: Don't Miss Europe's Re-Energized Defense Play
data.description:
Hensoldt (OTCPK:HAGHY, OTCPK:HNSDF) stock has surged 183% since I upgraded the rating to "Buy" in May 2024. While I believe that the company has substantial growth opportunities on a re-energized weapon systems recapitalization in Europe to drive autonomy on security and defense, I downgraded the stock to "Hold" in my June report as the stock price indicated fair valuation. Since then, the stock has remained flat, in line with my "Hold" rating. However, we note that the aerospace and defense peer group has significantly appreciated in value, and that may provide additional upside in combination with an upgraded outlook. In this report, I provide a fundamental assessment to test this thesis. I will also briefly assess the company’s most recent financial results and the upgraded financial outlook for 2025.

Hensoldt H1 Show Upward Pressure On Revenues And Reflect Cost Of Ramp-Up

Hensoldt saw its order intake increase 3% year-on-year, which is a modest increase. The company received €385 million worth of orders for the Eurofighter program, €300 million in orders for air defense radars, and €50 million in orders for ground-based systems sights. For the remainder of the year, the company expects around €200 million in orders for the Eurofighter, €200 million for radars, more than €500 million for optronics and self-protection systems for ground-based systems, more than €100 million for sensors, and €80 million for sustainment of the German P-8. So, that brings us to at least €1 billion in additional bookings. During the first half of the year, the backlog grew 8% to €7.1 billion, with 14% growth in core revenues to €896 million. It does show that while the company is accelerating sales, the backlog continues to grow. Book-to-bill during the quarter was 1.5x.

During the first half of the year, adjusted EBITDA grew modestly from €103 million to €107 million as margins dropped from 12.2% to 11.3%. Adjusted free cash flow dropped from negative €145 million to negative €181 million.

What we see in the results is that the company’s revenue growth is not reflected in the company’s EBITDA and cash flow. This is due to a backloaded free cash flow profile, product mix, and ramp-up of the new logistics center.

In the Sensors segment, core revenues grew 13% to €329 million, which is good considering that the company had a slow start to the year for radar production. I believe radars are also a high growth opportunity, as they play a key role in missile and air defense. EBITDA dropped from €117 million to €105 million due to a weaker product mix and the ramp-up of the logistics center. Order intake remained stable at around €1.26 billion, bringing the book-to-bill ratio to 1.53x.

The optronics business saw revenues grow 24% to €134 million. The South African part of the business saw slightly lower revenues, and the growth is actually realized in the German business. Adjusted EBITDA swung from a €14 million loss to a €1 million profit. This was driven by improving volumes in the German business and restructuring and repositioning of the South African business, showing modest signs of improvement. Order intake grew to €164 million, bringing the book-to-bill ratio to 1.2x.

So, Hensoldt has opportunities to grow its results in the sensors segment but also in the optronics segment. We note that Sensors is what currently brings in the money and is expected to show significant growth in the years ahead, but improving results in the optronics business are likely to provide a very positive layer moreover.

Hensoldt Updates Outlook For 2025

Hensoldt will be reporting third-quarter results in November. Ahead of the earnings report, the company has updated its guidance. Rather than a 1.2x book-to-bill ratio, the company now expects a book-to-bill ratio of 1.6x-1.9x, indicating a significant pipeline of orders expected to be finalized in H2. Revenue guidance decreased to approximately €2.5 billion from €2.5-€2.6 billion. So, part of the increase in the targeted book-to-bill ratio is driven by slightly lower revenues. Adjusted EBITDA margin guidance has improved from approximately 18% to 18% or higher.

Hensoldt Upside Expands On Peer Group Multiple Expansion

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Note: The price targets are based on the ordinary share prices. HAGHY represents 0.5 ordinary shares, so to get price targets for HAGHY simply divide the displayed price targets by two.

EBITDA estimates did not materially change. For 2025, the expectations have been revised downward by 2.5%, 0.6% down in 2026, and up 1.8% in 2027. CAGR measured from 2024 increased from 18% to 18.6%, but the overall change in EBITDA estimates between 2025 and 2027 is down 0.2%. Free cash flow estimates declined by 8.2% for this year, up 0.3% next year and up 1.7% in 2027. The CAGR increased from 44.6% to 45.3%, but the cumulative free cash flow generation between 2025 and 2027 has been revised down by 1.3%.

While the expectations for this year have been revised downward and mixed for next year, I am upgrading the stock from hold to buy. The change in estimates is not a driver of that revision. What we see is that the EV/EBITDA for aerospace and defense companies increased from 20.9x to 24x. The result is that whereas the stock appeared fairly valued in my prior assessment with FY26 earnings factored into the stock price, there now is 15% upside. That brings the base case stock price target to $131.19, and in a very bullish scenario it would get us to $161.90.

So, I am upgrading the stock from "Hold" to "Buy" on the expanded multiple, but we do note that in case peer group valuations drop, the stock price target for Hensoldt will also lose some support.

For investors interested in the underlying assumptions, I have added a quick overview sheet below:

Conclusion: Hensoldt Discount To Peer Make It Attractive

For Hensoldt, the changes in estimates to EBITDA and free cash flow did not drive the upside we see now. That was exclusively driven by the increase in the valuation multiples for aerospace and defense companies. However, with an upgrade to the expected book-to-bill ratio and recent security events in Europe with drone incursions, I believe demand and growth opportunities have been underlined once again, with upside against FY2026 earnings now visible. As a result, I feel comfortable upgrading the stock back to "Buy."

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
